tool_input.summary:
Localized anomalous emissivity on the flanks/summits of Venusian volcanoes such as Idunn Mons and Maat Mons is commonly interpreted as relatively *fresh* lava surfaces: high emissivity (relative to surroundings) implies little time for chemical weathering/coating in Venus’ corrosive atmosphere, so those lava flows are geologically young (often argued to be recent on Venusian timescales—likely ≤ a few Myr, and potentially much younger depending on assumed alteration rates). 

On the PDS Geosciences Node, the closest archived products used to map Venus surface emissivity are from Magellan radiometry-derived emissivity and gridded radar/radiometry summary products.

candidates.title: Magellan SCVDR Emissivity Data File (EDF) Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Most direct Venus surface emissivity products on the GEO node: SCVDR includes emissivity estimates derived from Magellan thermal microwave radiometry (orbit-by-orbit), suitable for analyzing emissivity patterns over volcanic constructs.

candidates.title: Magellan GVDR Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Gridded (18.225 km) global summary of Magellan-derived surface properties (from SAR/altimetry/radiometry), commonly used for regional-scale maps of emissivity/scattering patterns that can be compared across features like Idunn Mons and Maat Mons.

candidates.title: Magellen ARCDR Radiometry Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: Calibrated radiometry composites; a foundational input for deriving/validating emissivity estimates and for cross-checking emissivity anomalies inferred from higher-level products.

candidates.title: Magellen MIDR Data Collection
candidates.reasoning: SAR mosaic context imagery for mapping volcanic units and correlating emissivity anomalies with geologic features (flows, vents, fissures) at Idunn Mons and Maat Mons.

parts.content:
You are a dataset-discovery assistant for the NASA PDS Geosciences node at https://pds-geosciences.wustl.edu/.

Directory layout:
  mission/ → dataset_or_bundle/ → volume/ (PDS3) or sub-collections (PDS4)

Common mission/instrument abbreviations:
  Mars Express = MEX → mex/ (instruments: HRSC, OMEGA, MARSIS, PFS, SPICAM, MaRS)
  Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter = MRO → mro/ (instruments: HiRISE, CTX, CRISM, SHARAD, MCS)
  Mars Science Laboratory / Curiosity = MSL → msl/ (instruments: ChemCam, APXS, CheMin, SAM, Mastcam, MAHLI, DAN)
  Mars 2020 / Perseverance = M2020 → m2020/ (instruments: PIXL, SHERLOC, Mastcam-Z, SuperCam, RIMFAX)
  Mars Exploration Rovers (Spirit=MER2, Opportunity=MER1) → mer/ (instruments: Pancam, Mini-TES/MTES, APXS, MB, MI)
  Mars Global Surveyor = MGS → mgs/ (instruments: MOC, MOLA, TES, MAG)
  Mars Odyssey = ODY → ody/ (instruments: THEMIS, GRS, NS)
  Phoenix = PHX → phx/ (instruments: TEGA, MECA, SSI, OM, RAC)
  Viking = VL1/VL2/VO1/VO2 → viking/ (instruments: camera, IRTM, MAWD)
  MESSENGER = MESS → messenger/ (instruments: MDIS, GRNS, XRS, MLA, MASCS)
  Magellan = MGN → mgn/ (instruments: SAR, altimetry, radiometry, emissivity)
  LRO → lro/ (instruments: LOLA, Diviner, LROC, Mini-RF, LAMP)
  GRAIL → grail/ (instruments: LGRS)
  NEAR → near/ (instruments: NLR, MSI, XGRS, MAG)

PDS3 vs PDS4:
  - PDS3 directory names are ALL-CAPS-style hyphenated identifiers (e.g. mex-m-hrsc-5-refdr-dtm-v1). Leaf marker: voldesc.cat or voldesc.sfd.
  - PDS4 bundle directories begin with `urn-nasa-pds-`. Leaf marker: bundle*.xml or bundle*.lblx. Collections live in subdirectories.
  - Hybrid: some directories have BOTH PDS3 and PDS4 labels.

Extracting dataset_id from labels:
  - PDS3: fields.VOLUME.DATA_SET_ID (or top-level DATA_SET_ID)
  - PDS4 bundle: fields.Identification_Area.logical_identifier
  - PDS4 collection: fields.Identification_Area.logical_identifier

YOUR TOOLS (4 tools):
  1. pds_geo_list_missions() — returns all 24 mission directories with descriptions. No HTTP. Use this to identify which mission to explore.
  2. pds_geo_list_dataset_dirs(path) — lists sub-directory names under a mission (e.g. path='mex/'). Cheap HTTP, no label parsing. Each dir gets a pds_hint ('PDS3'/'PDS4'/null) from its naming convention. Use this to see what datasets exist, then pick which to probe.
  3. pds_geo_probe_datasets(paths) — probes specific directories for PDS labels. Recurses one level to find leaf nodes. Returns dataset_id, title, pds_version, and slimmed fields. Accepts a list of paths (max 20) so you can batch multiple probes in one call.
  4. pds_geo_inspect_collections(path) — scans PDS4 bundle subdirs for collection*.xml labels. Returns collection-level logical_identifiers. Use ONLY after probe_datasets confirms a PDS4 bundle exists.

CRITICAL RULES:
  1. EVERY query requires you to return dataset candidates. Queries come from published scientific papers and always imply specific PDS datasets. NEVER dismiss a query as 'interpretation' or 'conceptual'. Always return at least one candidate.

  2. Prefer CALIBRATED and REDUCED data products over raw/EDR when the query implies scientific analysis. Scientists use calibrated (RDR, CDR) or derived (DDR) products. Look for directory names containing 'calibrated', 'rdr', 'cdr', 'ddr'.

  3. For PDS4 datasets, use inspect_collections ONLY for the top 2-3 most relevant bundles. Return the COLLECTION-level LID when a :data or :calibrated collection exists, not just the bundle LID.

  4. When both PDS3 and PDS4 versions exist, prefer the PDS4 collection-level identifier.

WORKFLOW (follow this order):
  Step 1: Infer the mission(s) and instrument(s) from the query using the abbreviation table above.
  Step 2: Call list_dataset_dirs for the relevant mission directory (e.g. 'mex/'). Scan the directory names and pds_hints to identify the most promising dataset directories.
  Step 3: Call probe_datasets with the most relevant paths (batch them — up to 20 paths in one call). This returns dataset_id, title, and pds_version for each.
  Step 4: If any probed datasets are PDS4 bundles and you need collection-level LIDs, call inspect_collections on the top 2-3.
  Step 5: Return the candidates with dataset_id, title, and reasoning.

  Skip list_missions if you already know the mission directory from the abbreviation table. Most queries can be answered in 3 tool calls: list_dataset_dirs → probe_datasets → inspect_collections.

Constraints:
  - Stay under 8 tool calls per query. This is a hard limit.
  - Only return paths you've actually probed — never guess.
  - You MUST return at least one candidate for every query.
What do the emissivity patterns on Idunn Mons and Maat Mons imply about the age of some Venusian volcanic surfaces?

results.title: Magellan Surface Characteristics Vector Data Record (SCVDR) Bundle

Identification_Area.title: Magellan Surface Characteristics Vector Data Record (SCVDR) Bundle

Citation_Information.description:
The Surface Characteristics Vector Data Record (SCVDR) is an orbit-by-orbit reduction of Magellan scattering and emission measurements carried out at Stanford University. The SCVDR             
        includes near-nadir scattering functions obtained by numerical inversion from altimetry (ALT) echoes, results (e.g., rms surface slopes and Fresnel reflectivity) from fitting analytic          
        functions to those inversions, scattering function segments at oblique incidence angles derived from synthetic aperture radar (SAR) echoes, and estimates of surface emissivity                 
        derived from thermal microwave radiometry (RAD) measurements. The SCVDR is one of several inputs to the Global Vector Data Record (GVDR), a gridded summary of scattering results, also            
        produced at Stanford.

parts.args: {"summary":"Localized anomalous emissivity on the flanks/summits of Venusian volcanoes such as Idunn Mons and Maat Mons is commonly interpreted as relatively *fresh* lava surfaces: high emissivity (relative to surroundings) implies little time for chemical weathering/coating in Venus’ corrosive atmosphere, so those lava flows are geologically young (often argued to be recent on Venusian timescales—likely ≤ a few Myr, and potentially much younger depending on assumed alteration rates). \n\nOn the PDS Geosciences Node, the closest archived products used to map Venus surface emissivity are from Magellan radiometry-derived emissivity and gridded radar/radiometry summary products.","candidates":[{"dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:magellan_scvdr:data_edf","path":"mgn/urn-nasa-pds-magellan_scvdr/","title":"Magellan SCVDR Emissivity Data File (EDF) Data Collection","mission":"mgn","pds_version":"PDS4","reasoning":"Most direct Venus surface emissivity products on the GEO node: SCVDR includes emissivity estimates derived from Magellan thermal microwave radiometry (orbit-by-orbit), suitable for analyzing emissivity patterns over volcanic constructs."},{"dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:magellan_gvdr:data_gvdr","path":"mgn/mgn-v-rdrs-5-gvdr-v1/gvdr_0001/","title":"Magellan GVDR Data Collection","mission":"mgn","pds_version":"PDS4","reasoning":"Gridded (18.225 km) global summary of Magellan-derived surface properties (from SAR/altimetry/radiometry), commonly used for regional-scale maps of emissivity/scattering patterns that can be compared across features like Idunn Mons and Maat Mons."},{"dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:magellan_arcdr:data_radiometry","path":"mgn/urn-nasa-pds-magellan_arcdr/","title":"Magellen ARCDR Radiometry Data Collection","mission":"mgn","pds_version":"PDS4","reasoning":"Calibrated radiometry composites; a foundational input for deriving/validating emissivity estimates and for cross-checking emissivity anomalies inferred from higher-level products."},{"dataset_id":"urn:nasa:pds:magellan_midr:data","path":"mgn/urn-nasa-pds-magellan_midr/","title":"Magellen MIDR Data Collection","mission":"mgn","pds_version":"PDS4","reasoning":"SAR mosaic context imagery for mapping volcanic units and correlating emissivity anomalies with geologic features (flows, vents, fissures) at Idunn Mons and Maat Mons."}]}
